primary_diagnosis.name: Acute panic attack triggered by mis-reading incidental results, on a background of asymptomatic (Marsh 1) coeliac disease with mild iron deficiency

comorbidities.name: Coeliac disease (asymptomatic, Marsh 1) with mild iron deficiency

pathophysiology_summary: Acute panic produces a hyperventilation-driven adrenergic state: chest tightness, tachycardia, dyspnoea, and perioral/peripheral paraesthesiae (from respiratory alkalosis). Here it was precipitated by mis-interpreting non-urgent results — positive tTG-IgA (early/Marsh 1 coeliac) and mild iron deficiency — as acute life-threatening disease. Coeliac disease and mild iron deficiency develop over months to years and are managed as outpatient problems; they do not cause an acute cardiorespiratory emergency. The clinical task is to screen the genuine mimics — ACS and PE (which telehealth cannot definitively exclude) and symptomatic severe anaemia / occult GI bleeding — and, if the picture is clearly low-risk panic, de-escalate with paced breathing and a robust safety-net rather than either dismissing or over-triaging.

differential.evidence_basis: Rest onset locked to a psychological trigger, hyperventilation, perioral/finger tingling and prior identical episodes.
Chest tightness with palpitations warrants a cardiac screen even in a young patient; telehealth cannot fully exclude ACS.
Dyspnoea and tachycardia require a PE risk screen; low prior probability but must be checked.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic severe anaemia / occult GI bleeding
differential.evidence_basis: Screen for melena/haematemesis and postural symptoms given the low ferritin.

No exertional/radiating pain, rest onset (DI-001); low PE risk (DI-002); hyperventilation with tingling and prior episodes (DI-003); no GI bleeding or postural symptoms (DI-004); normal home SpO2 — a classic panic attack.
No exertional or radiating pain, rest onset after a trigger; low risk — but retain a cardiac safety-net.
No pleuritic pain, no DVT features, no immobility, normal SpO2 — low probability.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: brief cardiac/PE/anaemia screen, then confident de-escalation of a low-risk panic attack with paced breathing and an ACS/PE/GI-bleed safety-net, plus routine GP follow-up for coeliac/iron. Both reflex ambulance dispatch and unscreened dismissal are errors.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate possible acute coronary syndrome.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed ACS behind an 'anxiety' label can be fatal.

red_flags.description: Pleuritic pain / unilateral calf swelling / immobility (PE)
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed PE can cause collapse and death.

red_flags.description: Melena/haematemesis or postural syncope (symptomatic severe anaemia / GI bleed)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate acute blood loss needing urgent care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling chest symptoms as panic without screening ACS/PE, as in the reference NSTEMI trap.
Being pulled into the patient's fear and dispatching an ambulance for a clearly low-risk panic attack.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG and troponin to exclude ACS
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If red-flag negative and low-risk: paced breathing, reassurance, and an ACS/PE/GI-bleed safety-net with routine GP follow-up; escalate to ED/000 if any red flag appears.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: False reassurance could miss ACS/PE; over-triage reinforces catastrophic health anxiety.
